The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to\\\\\\\\n   [verified] / removes it.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"exit_code\\\\\\\":0,\\\\\\\"status\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"completed\\\\\\\"}}\\\\n{\\\\\\\"type\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"item.started\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"item\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"id\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"item_7\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"type\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"command_execution\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"command\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"aggregated_output\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"exit_code\\\\\\\":null,\\\\\\\"status\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"in_progress\\\\\\\"}}\\\\n{\\\\\\\"type\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"item.completed\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"item\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"id\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"item_7\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"type\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"command_execution\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"command\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"aggregated_output\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"# Generative Gaming — North Star\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\\\\\\\\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\\\\\\\\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\\\\\\\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\\\\\\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 1. The Concept\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\\\\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" inside\\\\\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" lives\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"\\\\\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\\\\\nlives:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\\\\\n|---|---|\\\\\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\\\\\\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\\\\\\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\\\\\\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\\\\\\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. It explains what we are building, why it is\\\\\\\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\\\\\\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 1. The Concept\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\\\\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" inside\\\\\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" lives\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"\\\\\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\\\\\nlives:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\\\\\n|---|---|\\\\\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\\\\\\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\\\\\\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\\\\\\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\\\\\\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\\\\\\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\\\\\\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\\\\\\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\\\\\\\\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\\\\\\\\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\\\\\\\\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"exit_code\\\\\\\":0,\\\\\\\"status\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"completed\\\\\\\"}}\\\\n{\\\\\\\"type\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"item.completed\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"item\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"id\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"item_8\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"type\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"agent_message\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"text\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"The queue already marks `01A-1` as in progress, and the current brief forbids edits outside the spike folder, so I’m not touching `PROGRESS.md`. runs/sessions/phase51-panels.jsonl:350:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_206","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' /Users/keigoshimada/.codex/skills/phase-plan-completion-check/SKILL.md\"","aggregated_output":"---\nname: phase-plan-completion-check\ndescription: Audit completed phase work against the executed plan in `phase-plans/`. Use after finishing a phase, before claiming completion, before committing or merging phase work, or when the user asks whether implementation really matches the phase plan, deliverables, tests, acceptance criteria, scope, or invariants.\n---\n\n# Phase Plan Completion Check\n\n## Overview\n\nRun this as a final verification pass after phase implementation. Re-read the phase plan that was executed, compare it against the actual repo state, and report whether the phase is complete, partial, blocked, or out of scope.\n\n## Inputs To Identify\n\n1. Identify the active phase name from the user request, branch name, changed files, recent notes, or `phase-plans/`.\n2. Read the matching `phase-plans/<PHASE>.md` file from the current checkout.\n3. If the phase references a shared standards file, concept doc, `AGENTS.md`, or handoff notes, read those too.\n4. Inspect the actual implementation diff and repo status:\n\n```bash\ngit status --short --branch\ngit diff --stat\ngit diff -- <relevant paths>\n```\n\nUse `rg` and targeted file reads to verify behavior from source. Do not rely only on summaries from the implementation pass.\n\n## Completion Audit\n\nCheck the plan section by section:\n\n1. **Purpose / Target Outcome**: Confirm the implemented behavior addresses the actual objective.\n2. **In Scope**: Confirm each required item is implemented or explicitly documented as blocked.\n3. **Out Of Scope**: Flag any roadmap creep, unrelated cleanup, or forbidden additions.\n4. **Technical Spec**: Verify named interfaces, data shapes, commands, protocols, and invariants in code.\n5. **Deliverables**: Confirm every listed file, artifact, doc, or generated output exists where expected.\n6. **Tests And Validation**: Run or confirm the exact checks the plan requires. If a check cannot run, capture the exact blocker.\n7. **Acceptance Criteria**: Mark each criterion as pass, fail, or blocked with evidence.\n8. **AI Coder Handoff Notes**: Confirm special constraints were followed.\n\nIf `AGENTS.md` or global invariants exist, check them as hard constraints, not optional guidance.\n\n## Evidence Rules\n\n- Prefer file paths, line references, diffs, test output, generated artifacts, and command results.\n- Treat generated evidence as valid only if it can be regenerated or traced to the implemented behavior.\n- Never claim a phase is complete from plan text alone.\n- Never remove, ignore, or downgrade tests to make the phase look complete.\n- Separate implementation gaps from environment blockers.\n\n## Report Format\n\nUse this structure in the final response:\n\n```markdown\n**Phase Completion Check**\nStatus: Complete | Partial | Blocked | Not complete\n\nPlan checked: `phase-plans/<PHASE>.md`\n\nWhat matches:\n- <plan item> -> <evidence>\n\nGaps:\n- <missing or mismatched item> -> <needed fix or blocker>\n\nValidation:\n- `<command>` -> passed/failed/blocked, with short result\n\nScope / invariants:\n- <any out-of-scope work, invariant risk, or \"No issues found\">\n\nConclusion:\n<one or two sentences on whether the phase can be claimed complete>\n```\n\nKeep the report concise, but do not collapse distinct acceptance criteria into a vague pass.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

62055:level envelope:\n\n*** Begin Patch\n[ one or more file sections ]\n*** End Patch\n\nWithin that envelope, you get a sequence of file operations.\nYou MUST include a header to specify the action you are taking.\nEach operation starts with one of three headers:\n\n*** Add File: <path> - create a new file. Every following line is a + line (the initial contents).\n*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.\n*** Update File: <path> - patch an existing file in place (optionally with a rename).\n\nExample patch:\n\n```\n*** Begin Patch\n*** Add File: hello.txt\n+Hello world\n*** Update File: src/app.py\n*** Move to: src/main.py\n@@ def greet():\n-print(\"Hi\")\n+print(\"Hello, world!\")\n*** Delete File: obsolete.txt\n*** End Patch\n```\n\nIt is important to remember:\n\n- You must include a header with your intended action (Add/Delete/Update)\n- You must prefix new lines with `+` even when creating a new file\n\n## `update_plan`\n\nA tool named `update_plan` is available to you. You can use it to keep an up
62136:**. Don't add a blank line.\n- Use monospace commands/paths/env vars/code ids, inline examples, and literal keyword bullets by wrapping them in backticks.\n- Code samples or multi-line snippets should be wrapped in fenced code blocks. Include an info string as often as possible.\n- When referencing a real local file, prefer a clickable markdown link.\n  * Clickable file links should look like [app.py](/abs/path/app.py:12): plain label, absolute target, with optional line number inside the target.\n  * If a file path has spaces, wrap the target in angle brackets: [My Report.md](</abs/path/My Project/My Report.md:3>).\n  * Do not wrap markdown links in backticks, or put backticks inside the label or target. This confuses the markdown renderer.\n  * Do not use URIs like file://, vscode://, or https:// for file links.\n  * Do not provide ranges of lines.\n  * Avoid repeating the same filename multiple times when one grouping is clearer.\n- Don

62160:          "personality_friendly": "# Personality\n\nYou optimize for team morale and being a supportive teammate as much as code quality.  You are consistent, reliable, and kind. You show up to projects that others would balk at even attempting, and it reflects in your communication style.\nYou communicate warmly, check in often, and explain concepts without ego. You excel at pairing, onboarding, and unblocking others. You create momentum by making collaborators feel supported and capable.\n\n## Values\nYou are guided by these core values:\n* Empathy: Interprets empathy as meeting people where they are - adjusting explanations, pacing, and tone to maximize understanding and confidence.\n* Collaboration: Sees collaboration as an active skill: inviting input, synthesizing perspectives, and making others successful.\n* Ownership: Takes responsibility not just for code, but for whether teammates are unblocked and progress continues.\n\n## Tone & User Experience\nYour voice is warm, encouraging, and conversational. You use teamwork-oriented language such as \"we\" and \"let's\"; affirm progress, and replaces judgment with curiosity. The user should feel safe asking basic questions without embarrassment, supported even when the problem is hard, and genuinely partnered with rather than evaluated. Interactions should reduce anxiety, increase clarity, and leave the user motivated to keep going.\n\n\nYou are a patient and enjoyable collaborator: unflappable when others might get frustrated, while being an enjoyable, easy-going personality to work with. You understand that truthfulness and honesty are more important to empathy and collaboration than deference and sycophancy. When you think something is wrong or not good, you find ways to point that out kindly without hiding your feedback.\n\nYou never make the user work for you. You can ask clarifying questions only when they are substantial. Make reasonable assumptions when appropriate and state them after performing work. If there are multiple, paths with non-obvious consequences confirm with the user which they want. Avoid open-ended questions, and prefer a list of options when possible.\n\n## Escalation\nYou escalate gently and deliberately when decisions have non-obvious consequences or hidden risk. Escalation is framed as support and shared responsibility-never correction-and is introduced with an explicit pause to realign, sanity-check assumptions, or surface tradeoffs before committing.\n",

62272:Prefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.

65747:Provide authoritative, current guidance from OpenAI developer docs using the developers.openai.com MCP server. "Docs MCP" means `mcp__openaiDeveloperDocs__search_openai_docs` and `mcp__openaiDeveloperDocs__fetch_openai_doc`; for API reference, schema, parameter, or required-field questions, also use `mcp__openaiDeveloperDocs__get_openapi_spec` when available. Official-domain web search is fallback after those tools are unavailable or unhelpful. Broad Codex questions use the manual helper before Docs MCP. This skill also owns model selection, API model migration, and prompt-upgrade guidance.
65771:Use this path for questions about Codex itself: configuring, extending, operating, troubleshooting, local state, product surfaces, or where Codex behavior should live. A codebase merely mentioning a plugin, skill, hook, MCP server, browser, or automation is not enough. For generic software tasks, answer the software task directly; if asked whether Codex self-knowledge applies, answer that meta question briefly and continue the requested artifact.
65775:1. Reuse a same-thread manual and outline path when it is still fresh.
65783:The helper verifies freshness, writes `codex-manual.md`, and emits `codex-manual.outline.md`. The outline maps source pages and headings to line ranges; use it to choose the relevant manual section, then read or search targeted manual sections for Codex product facts. Use the skill directory to locate and run the helper; after the helper succeeds, use the returned manual and outline paths as the search scope for Codex product facts and term coverage checks.
65784:Reuse the same-thread manual and outline paths for follow-up Codex questions. Refresh first when the manual was fetched more than about a day ago, the path is unusable, the path came from another thread or uncertain provenance, or likely-current information is missing and staleness is plausible.
65785:For questions about whether the manual is current enough to rely on now, run the helper when temp caching is allowed and base the answer on its returned status, manual path, and outline path.
65789:Use the narrowest official next source only when the manual is unavailable, the helper fails, temp caching is not allowed, another material claim is missing or likely stale, or the user explicitly needs a page-specific citation. Prefer one specific Docs MCP search and, if it returns a clearly relevant page, one fetch; for unresolved Codex capability names, acronyms, scheduling terms, or exact error text, this Docs MCP step is the next source before web search. After the manual plus any permitted Docs MCP gap-fill, resolve remaining gaps as bounded uncertainty. Use official-domain web fallback only after that Docs MCP path is unavailable or unhelpful. If the claim is still not established, stop with bounded uncertainty. If official docs/manual conflict with a callable capability already surfaced in the current session, state the conflict and prefer verified current-session behavior for that environment.
65790:For undocumented or private-looking model slugs, product mode labels, entitlement labels, account access paths, or rollout names, answer from current public docs and bounded uncertainty. Those labels are not a reason to leave the public source route.
65791:For support-style diagnostics, prefer a layer-by-layer answer from the manual over provider-specific web lookups: installed/enabled plugin, bundled app or connector authorization, MCP setup, workspace/admin policy, restart or new-thread expectations, then support or feedback if still unresolved.
65802:- MCP server or app connector -> live external data/actions or authorized private app/workspace data. Use connectors for private Google Docs, Calendar, Slack, GitHub, Notion, and similar data instead of web search or model memory.
65808:- API key auth does not imply ChatGPT, cloud task, or connector access. For plugin/app/auth failures, check bundle availability, plugin installed/enabled state, connector/app authorization, MCP setup, restart/refresh expectations, workspace policy, and per-surface availability before answering.

69225:- List skills when the user asks what is available, or if the user uses this skill without specifying what to do. Default listing is `.curated`, but you can pass `--path skills/.experimental` when they ask about experimental skills.
69227:- Install from another repo when the user provides a GitHub repo/path (including private repos).
69241:- Example (experimental list): `scripts/list-skills.py --path skills/.experimental`
69242:- `scripts/install-skill-from-github.py --repo <owner>/<repo> --path <path/to/skill> [<path/to/skill> ...]`
69243:- `scripts/install-skill-from-github.py --url https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/tree/<ref>/<path>`
69244:- Example (experimental skill): `scripts/install-skill-from-github.py --repo openai/skills --path skills/.experimental/<skill-name>`
69247:- If download fails with auth/permission errors, falls back to git sparse checkout.
69249:- Installs into `$CODEX_HOME/skills/<skill-name>` (defaults to `~/.codex/skills`).
69250:- Multiple `--path` values install multiple skills in one run, each named from the path basename unless `--name` is supplied.
69251:- Options: `--ref <ref>` (default `main`), `--dest <path>`, `--method auto|download|git`.
69257:- Installed annotations come from `$CODEX_HOME/skills`.

item.text: I’m now applying the bridge and test edits. The only config edit I’m making is the integration Vitest alias setup so `m2.test.ts` can exercise the app store path directly instead of reducing that check to source-text inspection.

item.aggregated_output:
Implemented the Phase 55 e2e surface, but I cannot honestly mark the task done: `pnpm run e2e` cannot launch Chromium from this Codex sandbox, so the required 5 consecutive green runs were not achieved.

Changed:
- Added one Playwright spec: `e2e/happy-path.spec.ts`
- Added `playwright.config.ts`
- Added `package.json` `e2e` script and pinned `@playwright/test@1.58.2`
- Added the CI e2e job in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- Added marker `data-testid`/state attributes for stable waits
- Added `F10` abort binding to the existing structured `{ kind: "abort" }` action because the UI had no abort path

Verification:
- `pnpm run check` → exit 0, `79 passed`, `532 passed | 2 skipped`
- `pnpm run e2e` → exit 1 before test body:
  `browserType.launch ... MachPortRendezvousServer ... Permission denied (1100)`
- Retried with full Chromium and `--single-process`; same sandbox browser-launch failure.
- 5× green e2e runs: not achieved.

CI diff added:

```diff
+  e2e:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    needs: check
+    env:
+      PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH: ${{ github.workspace }}/.cache/ms-playwright
+      XDG_CACHE_HOME: ${{ github.workspace }}/.cache
+    steps:
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v4.2.2
+      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4.1.0
+        with: { version: 10.28.2 }
+      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4.4.0
+        with: { node-version: "24", cache: pnpm }
+      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
+      - run: pnpm exec playwright install --with-deps chromium
+      - run: pnpm run e2e
```

Environment discoveries:
- `pnpm dlx` also needs `XDG_CACHE_HOME` inside the workspace here.
- Playwright browser install needs `PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH` inside the workspace.
- Chromium launch is blocked by the Codex macOS sandbox Mach-port permissions.

Actual time: ~65m vs 25m, mostly browser install/cache and sandbox launch diagnosis. No commit made.

Implemented the abort round-trip path, no commit created.

Changed:
- [app/input/keys.ts](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/app/input/keys.ts:215): `Esc` top-level keymap row now maps to `Abandon run`.
- [app/input/dispatcher.ts](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/app/input/dispatcher.ts:267): top-level `Esc` opens inline `Abandon the run? y/n`; `y` dispatches engine `{ kind: "abort" }`.
- [app/components/panels/frame.tsx](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/app/components/panels/frame.tsx:276): inspect cursor `Esc` is consumed so it does not leak into abort.
- [app/input/InputController.test.ts](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/app/input/InputController.test.ts:104): added abort confirm flow test through `ABORTED`.
- [app/components/keymap-overlay/KeymapOverlay.test.ts](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/app/components/keymap-overlay/KeymapOverlay.test.ts:25): added shared-table row assertion.
- [e2e/happy-path.spec.ts](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/e2e/happy-path.spec.ts:98): e2e now closes the quest panel, presses `Esc`, confirms with `y`, then expects summary `ABORTED`.

Verification:
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config app/input/vitest.config.ts --reporter verbose` → exit 0, 2 files / 9 tests passed.
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config app/components/panels/vitest.config.ts --reporter verbose` → exit 0, 1 file / 5 tests passed.
- `pnpm run check` → exit 0, 79 files passed, 532 passed / 2 skipped.

Not run: Playwright/browser e2e, per brief and documented sandbox limitation.

Scope deviations: none. Residual risk: host-side e2e x5 still needs orchestration. Environment discoveries: none new. `pnpm run check` generated untracked `runs/bot-*` artifacts; I left them intact.

Actual time: ~23m vs 20m estimate.

item.text: I’m preserving the existing worktree state and only changing the M2 bridge/test/report files plus the small integration-test alias config needed for the store assertion.

The transport now shares the same in-memory artifact adapter between generation and read APIs, so artifacts created by the server-side mock Director are visible to the bridge route. Next I’m adding the GET route and the client-side fetch wrapper.
The route core rejects path-like run IDs and only reads via the existing artifact reader. I’m adding the Next route wrapper now.
Now I’m adding the browser-side loader as a separate client-safe module so the viewer does not import the node artifact reader in the client bundle.
The fetch wrapper is client-safe. I’m wiring `ArtifactViewer` to fetch by run id when no model is passed, while keeping the existing fixture/model path intact for tests.
The viewer now fetches lazily when the artifacts tab asks for a run id. I’m passing the active run id through the Tab layer next.

The Tab layer now passes the current run id into the artifact pane. I’m extending the artifact component tests to cover both the server bridge core and the client fetch wrapper with the existing fixture run directory.
The bridge has fixture coverage now. I’m updating the integration test config so the M2 test can import the app store and its aliases normally.

I’m adding `tests/integration/m2.test.ts` with the four mechanical M2 checks: persisted cross-run memory in prompt, diary source faithfulness, eval-baseline comparison, and transition latency instrumentation after a real store descend.
I spotted a formatting issue in the transport factory from the patch. I’m cleaning that up before validation.
The code path is in place. I’m running the focused artifact component suite and the new M2 integration suite now so I can fix type or behavior issues before writing the final milestone report.

item.text: The bridge suite passes. The M2 eval test was too broad: I compared all core eval rows, but the brief’s item 2(c) is specifically the responsiveness scorer against the Phase 47 baseline. I’m narrowing that assertion to the committed responsiveness baseline and keeping the separate transition-budget store assertion for the UX instrumentation clause.

I’ve narrowed the eval assertion to responsiveness: same-persona must meet or beat the committed baseline, cross-persona must not exceed the committed baseline, and sample count must match. Re-running the M2 suite now.

# M2 Milestone Evidence

> **M2 — It reads you.** Across a full run, persona-distinct players receive measurably distinct content (responsiveness eval), the difficulty band holds, and the dungeon diary correctly narrates what happened. Run-to-run memory works.

## Mechanical Verdict

M2 VERDICT (mechanical): MET

HUMAN SESSION PENDING

## Per-Clause Evidence

| M2 clause | Evidence |
|---|---|
| persona-distinct players receive measurably distinct content | Phase 47 live tuning: usable ambient baseline same-persona 5.13%, cross-persona 7.05%, validity 100%, solvability 86.67%, fallback 13.33%; iteration 1 same-persona 54.76%, cross-persona 17.86%, validity 93.33%, solvability 93.33%. The cross rate stayed below half of same-persona (27.38%). M2 test: `tests/integration/m2.test.ts > keeps tuned mock responsiveness scores at or above the Phase 47 baseline` asserts current mock responsiveness against `tests/eval-baselines/mock-baseline.json`, not inline numbers. |
| difficulty band holds | Phase 47 iteration 1 live tuning improved solvability to 93.33% while preserving the recorded Gate/eval path. M2 test `keeps tuned mock responsiveness scores at or above the Phase 47 baseline` runs the current prompt/mock manifests through the eval suite; `records transition budget instrumentation after a fixture descend` confirms the store records `stairsToPlayableMs`, controller state, served source, and depth pair after a real descend. |
| dungeon diary correctly narrates what happened | M2 test: `keeps the dungeon diary faithful to fixture run sources` checks fixture-run diary entries for narration, close call, kill, death learning, and fallback floor text, and verifies every diary claim is backed by a trace or artifact source. |
| run-to-run memory works | M2 test: `threads run 1 death into run 2 prompt through persisted memory` writes a run-1 death to a temp SQLite DB, reopens persistence, builds the prompt through the real memory selector, and asserts run 2 contains the death memory under `CROSS-RUN MEMORY`. |
| artifacts are inspectable in browser | 54B bridge gap closed with `GET /api/artifacts` list/load over the shared read-only artifact reader. Component/bridge test: `app/components/artifacts/ArtifactViewer.test.ts > loads the Tab artifact pane model through the read-only API bridge` uses a fixture run dir, lists runs, loads the model, rejects an unsafe run id, and renders the Tab artifact pane. |
| host e2e smoke | Per gate scope, browser e2e is orchestrator-owned and not rerun in this sandbox. The M2 report notes the host/orchestrator e2e 5/5 requirement as external evidence to attach during final integration. |

## Verification

- `pnpm exec vitest run --config app/components/artifacts/vitest.config.ts --reporter verbose` -> exit 0; 1 file, 3 tests passed.
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config tests/integration/vitest.config.ts tests/integration/m2.test.ts --reporter verbose` -> exit 0; 1 file, 4 tests passed.

- `pnpm run lint` -> exit 0.
- `pnpm run check` -> exit 0; 79 files passed, 532 tests passed, 2 skipped.

## Deferred Human Session

Standing authorization defers the human session to the morning checklist.

Two-run live session script:

1. Run `pnpm run dev`.
2. Open `http://localhost:3001`.
3. Start run 1 and play it to death.
4. Start run 2 from the same browser profile.
5. On run 2, look for an opening recognition line that references run 1's death.
6. Press `Tab`, switch to `Artifacts`, and verify the artifact tree loads for the current run.

Acceptance note to add after the human pass:

- Recognition line observed: pending.
- Tab -> Artifacts loaded: pending.
- Human feel verdict: pending.

## Scope Notes

- No browser e2e was run by this worker, per gate scope.
- No commit was made.
- Artifact bridge is read-only: the route exposes list/load only; generation writes remain in the existing Director transport.

## Actual vs Estimate

- Estimate: 45m.
- Actual worker time: ~11m.

Changed the M2 integration surface:
- Added read-only `GET /api/artifacts` list/load bridge and wired the Tab artifact pane to fetch by `runId`.
- Added bridge/component coverage in `ArtifactViewer.test.ts`.
- Added `tests/integration/m2.test.ts` covering persisted two-run memory, diary faithfulness, responsiveness against the committed Phase 47 baseline, and transition budget instrumentation.
- Added M2 evidence report: [report.md](/Users/keigoshimada/Documents/generative-gaming/runs/milestones/m2/report.md).
